Exploring an Agent-Based Generative AI CAD Framework: Enhancing CAD Design Automation with AI-Driven Parametric Modelling and Adaptive Query Generation Valid Text Names: 'hakkak2025exploringanagentbased pages 1-7', 'hakkak2025exploringanagentbased pages 7-9', 'hakkak2025exploringanagentbased pages 9-14', 'hakkak2025exploringanagentbased pages 14-18', 'hakkak2025exploringanagentbased pages 18-21', 'hakkak2025exploringanagentbased pages 21-25', 'hakkak2025exploringanagentbased pages 25-29', 'hakkak2025exploringanagentbased pages 29-35', 'hakkak2025exploringanagentbased pages 35-39', 'hakkak2025exploringanagentbased pages 39-43', 'hakkak2025exploringanagentbased pages 43-48', 'hakkak2025exploringanagentbased pages 48-51', 'hakkak2025exploringanagentbased pages 51-56', 'hakkak2025exploringanagentbased pages 56-59', 'hakkak2025exploringanagentbased pages 59-68' BibTex: @article{hakkak2025exploringanagentbased, author = "Hakkak, A Aghaee and Ouzi, H Pourrasoul", title = "Exploring an Agent-Based Generative AI CAD Framework: Enhancing CAD Design Automation with AI-Driven Parametric Modelling and Adaptive Query Generation", year = "2025", journal = "Unknown journal" } Relevant Snippet: … on AI‑driven CAD, text‑to‑CAD pipelines, and agent‑based design frameworks, identifying both … To check feasibility quickly, we built a minimal prototype which was a small LLM, a single … --- ## 4. Agentic Large Language Models for Conceptual Systems Engineering and Design Valid Text Names: 'massoudi2025agenticlargelanguage pages 1-2', 'massoudi2025agenticlargelanguage pages 2-5', 'massoudi2025agenticlargelanguage pages 5-7', 'massoudi2025agenticlargelanguage pages 7-8', 'massoudi2025agenticlargelanguage pages 8-10', 'massoudi2025agenticlargelanguage pages 10-12' BibTex: @article{massoudi2025agenticlargelanguage, author = "Massoudi, Soheyl and Fuge, Mark", title = "Agentic Large Language Models for Conceptual Systems Engineering and Design", year = "2025", journal = "ArXiv", volume = "abs/2507.08619", month = "Jul", doi = "10.48550/arxiv.2507.08619", url = "https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2507.08619" } Abstract: Early-stage engineering design involves complex, iterative reasoning, yet existing large language model (LLM) workflows struggle to maintain task continuity and generate executable models. We evaluate whether a structured multi-agent system (MAS) can more effectively manage requirements extraction, functional decomposition, and simulator code generation than a simpler two-agent system (2AS). The target application is a solar-powered water filtration system as described in a cahier des charges. We introduce the Design-State Graph (DSG), a JSON-serializable representation that bundles requirements, physical embodiments, and Python-based physics models into graph nodes. A nine-role MAS iteratively builds and refines the DSG, while the 2AS collapses the process to a Generator-Reflector loop. Both systems run a total of 60 experiments (2 LLMs - Llama 3.3 70B vs reasoning-distilled DeepSeek R1 70B x 2 agent configurations x 3 temperatures x 5 seeds). We report a JSON validity, requirement coverage, embodiment presence, code compatibility, workflow completion, runtime, and graph size. Across all runs, both MAS and 2AS maintained perfect JSON integrity and embodiment tagging. Requirement coverage remained minimal (less than 20\%). Code compatibility peaked at 100\% under specific 2AS settings but averaged below 50\% for MAS. Only the reasoning-distilled model reliably flagged workflow completion. Powered by DeepSeek R1 70B, the MAS generated more granular DSGs (average 5-6 nodes) whereas 2AS mode-collapsed. Structured multi-agent orchestration enhanced design detail. Reasoning-distilled LLM improved completion rates, yet low requirements and fidelity gaps in coding persisted. Relevant Snippet: … LLM-powered multi-agent framework for engineering design, … tasks within a simple feedback iterative loop. FeaGPT: an End-to-End agentic-AI for Finite Element Analysis Valid Text Names: 'qi2510feagptanendtoend pages 1-2', 'qi2510feagptanendtoend pages 2-4', 'qi2510feagptanendtoend pages 4-7', 'qi2510feagptanendtoend pages 7-8', 'qi2510feagptanendtoend pages 8-10', 'qi2510feagptanendtoend pages 10-12', 'qi2510feagptanendtoend pages 12-14', 'qi2510feagptanendtoend pages 14-17', 'qi2510feagptanendtoend pages 17-19', 'qi2510feagptanendtoend pages 19-21' BibTex: @article{qi2510feagptanendtoend, author = "Qi, Yupeng and Xu, Ran and Chu, Xu", booktitle = "arXiv.org", journal = "ArXiv", title = "FeaGPT: an End-to-End agentic-AI for Finite Element Analysis", volume = "abs/2510.21993", year = "2510", month = "Oct", doi = "10.48550/arxiv.2510.21993", url = "https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2510.21993" } Relevant Snippet: … Multi-agent LLM systems have emerged as powerful tools for … integrated pipeline enables the system to handle tasks ranging from simple structural verification to comprehensive design … --- ## 7. From text to design: a framework to leverage LLM agents for automated CAD generation Valid Text Names: 'schupbach2025fromtextto pages 1-2', 'schupbach2025fromtextto pages 2-5', 'schupbach2025fromtextto pages 5-7', 'schupbach2025fromtextto pages 7-8', 'schupbach2025fromtextto pages 8-10', 'schupbach2025fromtextto pages 10-10' BibTex: @article{schupbach2025fromtextto, author = "Schüpbach, Aurel and Miguel, Raul San and Ferchow, Julian and Meboldt, Mirko", title = "From text to design: a framework to leverage LLM agents for automated CAD generation", year = "2025", journal = "Proceedings of the Design Society", volume = "5", pages = "1893-1902", month = "Aug", doi = "10.1017/pds.2025.10203", url = "https://doi.org/10.1017/pds.2025.10203", publisher = "Cambridge University Press (CUP)", issn = "2732-527X" } Abstract: ABSTRACT: Design generation using traditional Computer-Aided Design (CAD) tools remains a labor-intensive and manual task. This paper introduces a framework for automating CAD geometry generation using Large Language Models (LLMs) with function calling and agent workflows. The framework enables both expert and novice designers to use textual prompts to automatically generate CAD code. We evaluate it with five LLMs and four agent workflows. The agent workflow incorporating automated visual feedback outperforms the others, especially with multimodal LLMs like ChatGPT-4o. A case study shows its use in topology optimization and additive manufacturing with minimal human input. Remaining challenges include limitations in spatial reasoning, prompt dependency, and workflow adaptability. Future work should focus on improving design-for-manufacturing capabilities, visual tools, and evaluation benchmarking. Relevant Snippet: … visualInspection: This agent workflow adds a visual inspection … , incorporating an automated visual feedback loop, had a … enabling a fully automated design pipeline with minimal human … --- ## 8. iDesignGPT: large language model agentic workflows boost engineering design Valid Text Names: 'zhang2025idesigngptlargelanguage pages 1-4', 'zhang2025idesigngptlargelanguage pages 4-6', 'zhang2025idesigngptlargelanguage pages 6-9', 'zhang2025idesigngptlargelanguage pages 9-11', 'zhang2025idesigngptlargelanguage pages 11-14', 'zhang2025idesigngptlargelanguage pages 14-18', 'zhang2025idesigngptlargelanguage pages 18-24', 'zhang2025idesigngptlargelanguage pages 24-25' BibTex: @misc{zhang2025idesigngptlargelanguage, author = "Zhang, Zhinan and Liu, Songkai and Shen, Yanqing and Zhang, Yilun and Hou, Zhangli and Wang, Xin and Luo, Jianxi", title = "iDesignGPT: large language model agentic workflows boost engineering design", year = "2025", journal = "Unknown journal", month = "Jan", doi = "10.21203/rs.3.rs-5670522/v1", url = "https://doi.org/10.21203/rs.3.rs-5670522/v1", publisher = "Springer Science and Business Media LLC" } Abstract: Abstract

Engineering design, a cornerstone of technological innovation, faces persistent challenges from the rigidity of traditional methods and the insufficient responsiveness of emerging AI tools to fully address its inherently complex, dynamic, and creativity-driven demands. Here we introduce iDesignGPT, a novel framework that integrates large language model with established design methodologies to enable dynamic multi-agent collaboration for problem refinement, information gathering, design space exploration, and iterative optimization. By incorporating design metrics such as coverage, diversity, and novelty, iDesignGPT provides decision-enabling, data-driven insights for conceptual engineering design evaluation. Our results reveal that iDesignGPT surpasses benchmark models in generating innovative, modular, and rational solutions, particularly in exploratory, open-ended scenarios prioritizing creativity and adaptability. User studies, involving both students and experienced engineers, validate its ability to uncover hidden requirements, foster creativity, and enhance workflow transparency. Collectively, these findings position iDesignGPT as a scalable platform that lowers the expertise barrier, fosters interdisciplinary collaboration, and expands the transformative potential of AI-assisted engineering design.

function.arguments: {"question": "What generative CAD or deep learning CAD papers were published in ASME Journal of Mechanical Design (JMD) and JCISE between 2022-2025? What are representative recent papers?"}
function.name: gather_evidence

messages.content:
Most relevant evidence (with ids):

## pqac-00000017

From text 'daareyni2025generativeaimeets pages 9-10'.

Amirmohammad Daareyni, Antti Martikkala, Hossein Mokhtarian, and Iñigo Flores Ituarte. Generative ai meets cad: enhancing engineering design to manufacturing processes with large language models. The International Journal of Advanced Manufacturing Technology, Jun 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.1007/s00170-025-15830-2, doi:10.1007/s00170-025-15830-2. This article has 26 citations.

The Daareyni et al. (2025) paper presents GAD, an LLM-driven generative CAD framework that integrates CAD modeling, slicing, and manufacturing into a single user interface. The system was validated by comparing user-specified and final printed gear parameters, with observed deviations attributed to the manufacturing process, showing the framework follows design criteria. GAD can interpret images and reproduce general shapes, but the paper notes limitations in achieving fine-detail fidelity and precise dimensional accuracy, especially with limited input or complex geometries. The authors evaluated alternative mid-sized LLMs (Google Gemini 2.5 Pro and OlympicCoder) specifically "to assess their performance in generating SCAD code." Both showed high success rates but long execution times, highlighting trade-offs between model performance and processing efficiency. Future work suggested includes better dimensional extrapolation, handling complex geometries, advanced error-detection/self-correction, and exploring cost- or speed-efficient LLM alternatives or locally trained models. The article references OpenSCAD and several practical tools (Streamlit, Slic3r, Printrun), is published in The International Journal of Advanced Manufacturing Technology, is open-access under a CC BY 4.0 license, and the record notes "This article has 26 citations."


---

## pqac-00000018

From text 'daareyni2025generativeaimeets pages 1-2'.

Amirmohammad Daareyni, Antti Martikkala, Hossein Mokhtarian, and Iñigo Flores Ituarte. Generative ai meets cad: enhancing engineering design to manufacturing processes with large language models. The International Journal of Advanced Manufacturing Technology, Jun 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.1007/s00170-025-15830-2, doi:10.1007/s00170-025-15830-2. This article has 26 citations.

The Daareyni et al. (2025) paper, published in The International Journal of Advanced Manufacturing Technology, presents a framework for LLM-assisted CAD that explicitly uses an advanced large language model (GPT-4o) to let users generate and manipulate 3D models from multimodal inputs. The framework accepts text, images (e.g., blueprints), and voice, integrates image recognition and voice detection, and translates user inputs into CAD code; it also employs a closed feedback loop to iteratively refine generated models to match user specifications. The paper positions this approach as lowering the barrier for non-professionals and easing design-to-manufacturing workflows. The excerpt lists keywords tying the work to Computer-aided design, Design automation, AI, and LLMs, and shows standard journal metadata (DOI, received/accepted dates), indicating IJAMT is a venue that publishes applied research at the intersection of AI, CAD, and manufacturing. The excerpt does not mention any specific CAD implementation such as OpenSCAD.

function.arguments:
{"artifact_type": "table", "description": "Create a comprehensive ranked table of publication venues for a generative-CAD + open-hardware SDL paper. The table should be sorted by overall fit for an APPLIED hardware/SDL contribution that USES generative-CAD methods (LLM-driven code-based CAD via CadQuery/OpenSCAD/build123d for a multi-powder doser for autonomous AM alloy discovery). Include columns for:
1. Rank (1-best fit overall)
2. Venue full name
3. Publisher
4. Approximate Impact Factor / CiteScore / h5-index
5. Scope summary (1 sentence)
6. Accepts applied hardware/SDL contribution? (Yes/No/Partial)
7. Acceptance bar (High/Medium/Low)
8. Representative recent generative-CAD paper (2022-2025)
9. Fit justification (1-2 sentences)

Include these venues in the ranking:
- Tier 1 RECOMMENDED: Digital Discovery (RSC), ASME Journal of Mechanical Design, JCISE, International Journal of Advanced Manufacturing Technology, Additive Manufacturing, Advanced Engineering Informatics, Computers in Industry, Computer-Aided Design (Elsevier)
- Tier 2 POSSIBLE but harder: Proceedings of the Design Society, Computer-Aided Design and Applications, Applied Sciences, HardwareX
- Tier 3 HIGH-IMPACT BUT LIKELY NOT SUITABLE for applied work: ACM Transactions on Graphics (SIGGRAPH), CVPR, NeurIPS, ICML, ICLR, ICCV/ECCV

Sort primarily by fit for the applied contribution, with impact factor as a secondary factor."}
function.name: create_artifact

messages.content:
### Artifact (ID artifact-00)

| Rank | Venue full name | Publisher | Approximate Impact Factor / CiteScore / h5-index | Scope summary | Accepts applied hardware/SDL contribution? | Acceptance bar | Representative recent generative-CAD paper (2022-2025) | Fit justification |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Digital Discovery | Royal Society of Chemistry (RSC) | IF ~6.2; CiteScore ~10 | Publishes digital, automated, AI-enabled chemistry/materials workflows, including self-driving labs, open hardware, and low-cost automation. | Yes | Medium | Yoshikawa et al., “Digital pipette: open hardware for liquid transfer in self-driving laboratories” (2023); Doloi et al., “Democratizing Self-Driving Labs: Advances in Low-Cost 3D Printing for Laboratory Automation” (2025) (pqac-00000013, pqac-00000008) | Best overall fit if the paper foregrounds the open-hardware SDL contribution and treats generative-CAD as the enabling design methodology. It already publishes SDL hardware, 3D-printed lab automation, and community-facing open systems papers, so a multi-powder doser for autonomous AM alloy discovery is squarely in-scope. |
| 2 | Additive Manufacturing | Elsevier | IF ~11; CiteScore ~19 | Top AM journal covering materials, processes, design-for-AM, AM systems, and integrated design/manufacturing workflows. | Partial | High | AM-driven generative design / DfAM papers are common; e.g., ML-assisted DfAM and topology/generative-design studies cited in the AM literature review stream (Watson 2023 contextual fit via AM-focused generative design) | Highest-impact manufacturing venue still plausibly amenable if the paper emphasizes the doser as novel AM-enabling hardware for alloy discovery and shows that the generative-CAD pipeline materially improved printability, modularity, or DfAM performance. Less ideal if the manuscript is mostly about SDL architecture rather than AM outcomes. |
| 3 | The International Journal of Advanced Manufacturing Technology | Springer Nature | IF ~3.4; CiteScore ~8 | Broad manufacturing journal spanning design, process planning, automation, robotics, CAD/CAM, and applied AI in manufacturing. | Yes | Medium-High | Daareyni et al., “Generative AI meets CAD: enhancing engineering design to manufacturing processes with large language models” (2025); Watson et al., “Generative design of space frames for additive manufacturing technology” (2023) (pqac-00000017, pqac-00000018) | Very strong fit for a paper that integrates LLM-driven code-based CAD with actual fabrication hardware and manufacturing validation. It has direct precedent for OpenSCAD/LLM CAD pipelines and for generative-design-for-AM papers, making it one of the safest journal targets. |
| 4 | Journal of Mechanical Design | ASME | IF ~3.3; CiteScore ~6 | Flagship engineering-design journal focused on methods, theories, and systems for design synthesis, automation, and computational design. | Partial | High | Regenwetter et al., “Deep Generative Models in Engineering Design: A Review” (2022); GenCAD=3D (2025) noted in recent JMD-linked literature (pqac-00000019) | Excellent fit if the paper makes a genuine engineering-design-method contribution: e.g., an agentic generative-CAD workflow with verification, parameterized modularity, and DfAM-aware design rules validated on the doser. It is less suitable if the main novelty is the hardware build itself rather than the design methodology. |
| 5 | Journal of Computing and Information Science in Engineering (JCISE) | ASME | IF ~3.1; CiteScore ~6 | Covers computational methods, CAD/CAE/CAM, design automation, geometric computing, and AI/ML for engineering information and systems. | Partial | Medium-High | Recent JCISE/JMD-linked LLM4CAD and CAD-automation literature cluster; code-generation/engineering informatics framing fits the venue’s computational-design remit (pqac-00000019) | Strong choice when the core claim is a reusable computational pipeline for text/code-based CAD generation, verification, and design-space exploration, with the hardware as the case study. More amenable than pure ML venues, but still expects a clear computational contribution. |
| 6 | Computer-Aided Design | Elsevier | IF ~3.0; CiteScore ~7 | Core CAD journal for geometric modeling, design automation, computational representations, and CAD-related AI methods. | Partial | High | Simplification of 3D CAD models using GANs (2023, cited as unobtainable in search); broader generative-CAD lineage including B-rep/sequence modeling is central to the venue ecosystem (pqac-00000000, pqac-00000006) | Strong venue if the manuscript leans into the generative-CAD pipeline itself: code representations, geometry-kernel verification, parametric editability, and CAD automation. Weaker if the paper is mainly an SDL/open-hardware system paper with minimal CAD-method novelty. |
| 7 | Advanced Engineering Informatics | Elsevier | IF ~8.0; CiteScore ~15 | Publishes AI, knowledge-based systems, digital engineering, and informatics for design, construction, and manufacturing. | Yes | High | Generative/AI-for-design and engineering-informatics papers are common; recent agentic/LLM engineering design pipelines are highly consonant with venue norms | Very attractive if the paper stresses the end-to-end digital thread: LLM-driven CAD, validation, knowledge capture, hardware documentation, and closed-loop laboratory deployment. It is more systems-friendly than pure CAD journals, while still rewarding methodological novelty. |
| 8 | Computers in Industry | Elsevier | IF ~10.0; CiteScore ~17 | Focuses on industrially relevant computer-based methods, digitalization, automation, and deployable systems in engineering/manufacturing. | Yes | High | Applied AI/CAD/manufacturing systems papers are typical; recent industrial CAD-generation and automation work fits its translational orientation | Good high-impact option if the doser is presented as a deployable industrial-style digital engineering system rather than a purely academic design-method paper. Better for demonstrable workflow integration and usability than for algorithmic CAD novelty per se. |
| 9 | Proceedings of the Design Society | Cambridge University Press / Design Society | archival proceedings; no standard JIF; CiteScore modest | Archival design-research proceedings covering engineering design methods, tools, and emerging AI-enabled design workflows. | Yes | Medium | Schüpbach et al., “From text to design: a framework to leverage LLM agents for automated CAD generation” (2025) (pqac-00000016) | One of the clearest venues for agentic generative-CAD workflows with a real engineering case study. Lower impact than the best journals, but notably more amenable to an applied paper where the methodological contribution is modest yet meaningful. |
| 10 | Computer-Aided Design and Applications | CAD Solutions / U-turn Press | IF ~0.8; CiteScore ~2 | Application-oriented CAD journal publishing practical CAD methods, implementations, and case studies. | Yes | Medium-Low | Shao & Shao, “Innovation of Apparel Design Based on Deep Generative Modeling and Its Practice of CAD Collaborative Design” (2024) | Plausible outlet for a pragmatic code-based generative-CAD pipeline tied to a hardware case study, especially if the contribution is more implementation-oriented than theoretically deep. Lower prestige, but comparatively forgiving on applied systems work. |
| 11 | HardwareX | Elsevier | IF ~2.5; CiteScore ~4 | Dedicated open-hardware journal for detailed, reproducible scientific hardware descriptions with build files and validation. | Yes | Medium | Laborem Box (2022) and other open-source lab platforms; not generative-CAD-specific, but directly aligned with publishable hardware documentation (pqac-00000015) | Best venue if the primary goal is to disseminate the open hardware itself with files, BOM, and validation. However, it is not a high-impact generative-CAD venue, so it is better as a companion paper than as the main flagship publication for the CAD/AI angle. |
| 12 | Applied Sciences | MDPI | IF ~2.5; CiteScore ~4 | Broad applied-engineering journal that accepts practical AI/manufacturing/CAD applications across many domains. | Yes | Low-Medium | Yüksel & Börklü, “A generative deep learning approach for improving the mechanical performance of structural components” (2024, cited in search results) | Reasonably amenable to applied system papers and fast-moving AI/manufacturing work, but less selective and less field-defining. Useful fallback if the work is interdisciplinary but does not fit a sharper design/manufacturing venue. |
| 13 | ACM Transactions on Graphics (SIGGRAPH / SIGGRAPH Asia) | ACM | IF ~6.2; h5-index aligned with SIGGRAPH prestige | Premier graphics venue for fundamental advances in geometry, graphics, and 3D generation. | No | Very High | Xu et al., “BrepGen: A B-rep Generative Diffusion Model with Structured Latent Geometry” (2024) (pqac-00000006) | Extremely high prestige for generative-CAD methods, but a poor fit for an applied SDL hardware paper that merely uses such methods. Acceptance would require a substantial new generative geometry/modeling algorithm, not just a strong hardware deployment. |

function.arguments:
{"answer": "# Venue Analysis for a Generative-CAD / Open-Hardware Self-Driving Laboratory Contribution

## 1. The Publication Landscape for Generative-CAD Work

Generative-CAD research—including parametric sequence generation, B-rep synthesis, and LLM-driven text-to-CAD—is published across a broad spectrum of venues. Evidence from recent literature shows that the highest-profile generative-CAD *methods* papers appear in top ML and computer vision venues: CVPR hosted CAD-Llama (Li et al., 2025) for LLM-based parametric CAD generation (pqac-00000003), NeurIPS published Text2CAD (Khan et al., 2024) (pqac-00000001, pqac-00000005), ICML published SkexGen (Xu et al., 2022) (pqac-00000005, pqac-00000006), ICLR has OBJ2CAD-type work under review (pqac-00000007), and ACM Transactions on Graphics (SIGGRAPH) published BrepGen (Xu et al., 2024) (pqac-00000006). Applied code-based generative-CAD work using frameworks like CadQuery and OpenSCAD has appeared in engineering journals: Daareyni et al. (2025) published an LLM-to-OpenSCAD pipeline in The International Journal of Advanced Manufacturing Technology (pqac-00000017, pqac-00000018), Schüpbach et al. (2025) published an agentic LLM-CAD framework in Proceedings of the Design Society (pqac-00000016), and the Text-to-CadQuery system (Xie & Ju, 2025) demonstrates CadQuery-specific LLM fine-tuning (pqac-00000000, pqac-00000002).

Separately, self-driving laboratory (SDL) hardware contributions—particularly open-source, 3D-printed, and modular instruments—are concentrated in Digital Discovery (RSC), which has published open-hardware SDL platforms including the digital pipette (Yoshikawa et al., 2023) and democratized 3D-printed lab automation (Doloi et al., 2025) (pqac-00000008, pqac-00000013), as well as in HardwareX, which accepts detailed open-source hardware descriptions (pqac-00000015). Other outlets for SDL hardware include Nature Communications, Chemical Reviews, and domain-specific chemistry/materials journals (pqac-00000010, pqac-00000011, pqac-00000012).

## 2. The Core Trade-Off: Applied Hardware/SDL vs. Pure Methods

The critical trade-off for this project is that the contribution is primarily an *applied* hardware/SDL system paper—a multi-powder doser for autonomous AM alloy discovery—that *uses* generative-CAD (LLM/AI-driven code-based CAD via CadQuery/build123d/OpenSCAD, with agentic design loops and geometry-kernel verifiers) as a design methodology, rather than advancing generative-CAD methods per se. This distinction fundamentally shapes venue selection:

**Venues that would NOT accept the paper** (high-impact but methods-focused): ACM TOG/SIGGRAPH, CVPR, ICCV/ECCV, NeurIPS, ICML, and ICLR all publish generative-CAD work, but they require the primary contribution to be a novel ML, vision, or graphics *method*. An applied paper that uses LLM-CAD to design hardware but does not advance the underlying model architecture, training procedure, or representation would almost certainly be desk-rejected at these venues (pqac-00000003, pqac-00000006).

**Venues that WOULD accept the paper** (applied-systems-friendly): Digital Discovery, IJAMT, ASME JMD/JCISE, Additive Manufacturing, Advanced Engineering Informatics, Computers in Industry, and Proceedings of the Design Society all have track records of publishing applied AI-for-design or hardware/SDL contributions, provided there is sufficient methodological or engineering novelty (pqac-00000013, pqac-00000017, pqac-00000016, pqac-00000019).

## 3. Ranked Venue Table

The following table ranks all candidate venues by overall fit for the described contribution, with impact as a secondary factor.

<artifact id="artifact-00"/>

## 4. Top Recommendations

Based on the analysis, the recommended publication strategy depends on how the manuscript is framed:

### Primary Recommendation: Digital Discovery (RSC)
Digital Discovery is the single venue that sits at the intersection of SDL open hardware, AI-driven automation, and accessible 3D-printed instruments for materials discovery. It has published open-hardware SDL platforms (pqac-00000008, pqac-00000013, pqac-00000014), has a growing IF (~6.2), and would welcome a paper that foregrounds the multi-powder doser as an AI-designed, open-source SDL module for autonomous AM alloy discovery. The generative-CAD pipeline would serve as the enabling methodology rather than needing to be a standalone ML contribution.

### High-Impact Alternative: The International Journal of Advanced Manufacturing Technology (Springer)
IJAMT has direct precedent for LLM-driven code-based CAD work applied to manufacturing: Daareyni et al. (2025) published an LLM-to-OpenSCAD pipeline with manufacturing validation in this exact journal (pqac-00000017, pqac-00000018). A paper framing the doser as a case study in LLM-driven design-for-additive-manufacturing with fabrication and functional validation would be highly competitive here.

### If Emphasizing Design Methodology: ASME Journal of Mechanical Design or JCISE
If the manuscript can make a genuine methodological contribution regarding the agentic design loop, geometry-kernel verification, or DfAM-aware generative-CAD workflow, JMD or JCISE would provide higher prestige within the engineering design community. JMD has published the deep generative models review by Regenwetter et al. (2022) and GenCAD=3D (2025) (pqac-00000019), establishing precedent for AI-enabled design synthesis work.

### If Emphasizing Manufacturing/AM Outcomes: Additive Manufacturing (Elsevier)
With an IF of ~11, Additive Manufacturing is the highest-impact manufacturing-specific venue. However, the paper would need to center on AM-relevant contributions (novel powder dosing mechanisms, multi-material process capabilities, alloy discovery results) rather than the CAD pipeline itself.

### Complementary Hardware Paper: HardwareX
For maximum reproducibility impact, a companion HardwareX paper providing the full open-hardware documentation (CAD files, BOM, assembly instructions, validation data) would complement a more methodologically focused flagship paper in one of the venues above (pqac-00000015).

### Conference Option: Proceedings of the Design Society (ICED/DESIGN Conference)
For rapid dissemination, the Design Society conferences (ICED, DESIGN) accept applied LLM-CAD pipeline papers with engineering case studies, as demonstrated by Schüpbach et al. (2025) (pqac-00000016). This is lower-impact but highly amenable to the described contribution.

## 5. Venues to Avoid for This Paper

ACM Transactions on Graphics, CVPR, NeurIPS, ICML, ICLR, and ICCV/ECCV should be avoided unless the manuscript can be reframed around a fundamentally new generative-CAD method (e.g., a novel agentic architecture with broadly applicable improvements over existing text-to-CAD systems, validated on multiple design domains beyond the doser). As currently described—an applied hardware system using generative-CAD as a tool—these venues would reject the paper on scope grounds (pqac-00000003, pqac-00000005, pqac-00000006, pqac-00000007)."}

Separately, self-driving laboratory (SDL) hardware contributions—particularly open-source, 3D-printed, and modular instruments—are concentrated in Digital Discovery (RSC), which has published open-hardware SDL platforms including the digital pipette (Yoshikawa et al., 2023) and democratized 3D-printed lab automation (Doloi et al., 2025) (pqac-00000008, pqac-00000013), as well as in HardwareX, which accepts detailed open-source hardware descriptions (pqac-00000015). Other outlets for SDL hardware include Nature Communications, Chemical Reviews, and domain-specific chemistry/materials journals (pqac-00000010, pqac-00000011, pqac-00000012).

## 2. The Core Trade-Off: Applied Hardware/SDL vs. Pure Methods

The critical trade-off for this project is that the contribution is primarily an *applied* hardware/SDL system paper—a multi-powder doser for autonomous AM alloy discovery—that *uses* generative-CAD (LLM/AI-driven code-based CAD via CadQuery/build123d/OpenSCAD, with agentic design loops and geometry-kernel verifiers) as a design methodology, rather than advancing generative-CAD methods per se. This distinction fundamentally shapes venue selection:

**Venues that would NOT accept the paper** (high-impact but methods-focused): ACM TOG/SIGGRAPH, CVPR, ICCV/ECCV, NeurIPS, ICML, and ICLR all publish generative-CAD work, but they require the primary contribution to be a novel ML, vision, or graphics *method*. An applied paper that uses LLM-CAD to design hardware but does not advance the underlying model architecture, training procedure, or representation would almost certainly be desk-rejected at these venues (pqac-00000003, pqac-00000006).

**Venues that WOULD accept the paper** (applied-systems-friendly): Digital Discovery, IJAMT, ASME JMD/JCISE, Additive Manufacturing, Advanced Engineering Informatics, Computers in Industry, and Proceedings of the Design Society all have track records of publishing applied AI-for-design or hardware/SDL contributions, provided there is sufficient methodological or engineering novelty (pqac-00000013, pqac-00000017, pqac-00000016, pqac-00000019).

## 3. Ranked Venue Table

<artifact id="artifact-00"/>

## 4. Top Recommendations

Based on the analysis, the recommended publication strategy depends on how the manuscript is framed:

### Primary Recommendation: Digital Discovery (RSC)
Digital Discovery is the single venue that sits at the intersection of SDL open hardware, AI-driven automation, and accessible 3D-printed instruments for materials discovery. It has published open-hardware SDL platforms (pqac-00000008, pqac-00000013, pqac-00000014), has a growing IF (~6.2), and would welcome a paper that foregrounds the multi-powder doser as an AI-designed, open-source SDL module for autonomous AM alloy discovery. The generative-CAD pipeline would serve as the enabling methodology rather than needing to be a standalone ML contribution.

### High-Impact Alternative: The International Journal of Advanced Manufacturing Technology (Springer)
IJAMT has direct precedent for LLM-driven code-based CAD work applied to manufacturing: Daareyni et al. (2025) published an LLM-to-OpenSCAD pipeline with manufacturing validation in this exact journal (pqac-00000017, pqac-00000018). A paper framing the doser as a case study in LLM-driven design-for-additive-manufacturing with fabrication and functional validation would be highly competitive here.

### If Emphasizing Design Methodology: ASME Journal of Mechanical Design or JCISE
If the manuscript can make a genuine methodological contribution regarding the agentic design loop, geometry-kernel verification, or DfAM-aware generative-CAD workflow, JMD or JCISE would provide higher prestige within the engineering design community. With an IF of ~11, Additive Manufacturing is the highest-impact manufacturing-specific venue. However, the paper would need to center on AM-relevant contributions (novel powder dosing mechanisms, multi-material process capabilities, alloy discovery results) rather than the CAD pipeline itself.

### Complementary Hardware Paper: HardwareX
For maximum reproducibility impact, a companion HardwareX paper providing the full open-hardware documentation (CAD files, BOM, assembly instructions, validation data) would complement a more methodologically focused flagship paper in one of the venues above (pqac-00000015).

### Conference Option: Proceedings of the Design Society (ICED/DESIGN Conference)
For rapid dissemination, the Design Society conferences (ICED, DESIGN) accept applied LLM-CAD pipeline papers with engineering case studies, as demonstrated by Schüpbach et al. (2025) (pqac-00000016). This is lower-impact but highly amenable to the described contribution.

## 5. Venues to Avoid for This Paper

ACM Transactions on Graphics, CVPR, NeurIPS, ICML, ICLR, and ICCV/ECCV should be avoided unless the manuscript can be reframed around a fundamentally new generative-CAD method (e.g., a novel agentic architecture with broadly applicable improvements over existing text-to-CAD systems, validated on multiple design domains beyond the doser). As currently described—an applied hardware system using generative-CAD as a tool—these venues would reject the paper on scope grounds (pqac-00000003, pqac-00000005, pqac-00000006, pqac-00000007)."}

Other outlets for SDL hardware include Nature Communications, Chemical Reviews, and domain-specific chemistry/materials journals (pqac-00000010, pqac-00000011, pqac-00000012).

## 2. The Core Trade-Off: Applied Hardware/SDL vs. Pure Methods

The critical trade-off for this project is that the contribution is primarily an *applied* hardware/SDL system paper—a multi-powder doser for autonomous AM alloy discovery—that *uses* generative-CAD (LLM/AI-driven code-based CAD via CadQuery/build123d/OpenSCAD, with agentic design loops and geometry-kernel verifiers) as a design methodology, rather than advancing generative-CAD methods per se. This distinction fundamentally shapes venue selection:

**Venues that would NOT accept the paper** (high-impact but methods-focused): ACM TOG/SIGGRAPH, CVPR, ICCV/ECCV, NeurIPS, ICML, and ICLR all publish generative-CAD work, but they require the primary contribution to be a novel ML, vision, or graphics *method*. An applied paper that uses LLM-CAD to design hardware but does not advance the underlying model architecture, training procedure, or representation would almost certainly be desk-rejected at these venues (pqac-00000003, pqac-00000006).

**Venues that WOULD accept the paper** (applied-systems-friendly): Digital Discovery, IJAMT, ASME JMD/JCISE, Additive Manufacturing, Advanced Engineering Informatics, Computers in Industry, and Proceedings of the Design Society all have track records of publishing applied AI-for-design or hardware/SDL contributions, provided there is sufficient methodological or engineering novelty (pqac-00000013, pqac-00000017, pqac-00000016, pqac-00000019).

*Table: This table ranks peer-reviewed venues for a generative-CAD plus open-hardware self-driving-lab paper, prioritizing fit for an applied hardware/SDL contribution over raw venue prestige. It distinguishes journals and conferences that are realistically receptive from high-impact ML/graphics venues that publish generative CAD but would likely reject an applied systems paper.*

## 4. Top Recommendations

Based on the analysis, the recommended publication strategy depends on how the manuscript is framed:

### Primary Recommendation: Digital Discovery (RSC)
Digital Discovery is the single venue that sits at the intersection of SDL open hardware, AI-driven automation, and accessible 3D-printed instruments for materials discovery. It has published open-hardware SDL platforms (pqac-00000008, pqac-00000013, pqac-00000014), has a growing IF (~6.2), and would welcome a paper that foregrounds the multi-powder doser as an AI-designed, open-source SDL module for autonomous AM alloy discovery. The generative-CAD pipeline would serve as the enabling methodology rather than needing to be a standalone ML contribution.

### High-Impact Alternative: The International Journal of Advanced Manufacturing Technology (Springer)
IJAMT has direct precedent for LLM-driven code-based CAD work applied to manufacturing: Daareyni et al. (2025) published an LLM-to-OpenSCAD pipeline with manufacturing validation in this exact journal (pqac-00000017, pqac-00000018). A paper framing the doser as a case study in LLM-driven design-for-additive-manufacturing with fabrication and functional validation would be highly competitive here.

### If Emphasizing Design Methodology: ASME Journal of Mechanical Design or JCISE
If the manuscript can make a genuine methodological contribution regarding the agentic design loop, geometry-kernel verification, or DfAM-aware generative-CAD workflow, JMD or JCISE would provide higher prestige within the engineering design community. JMD has published the deep generative models review by Regenwetter et al. (2022) and GenCAD=3D (2025) (pqac-00000019), establishing precedent for AI-enabled design synthesis work.

### If Emphasizing Manufacturing/AM Outcomes: Additive Manufacturing (Elsevier)
With an IF of ~11, Additive Manufacturing is the highest-impact manufacturing-specific venue. However, the paper would need to center on AM-relevant contributions (novel powder dosing mechanisms, multi-material process capabilities, alloy discovery results) rather than the CAD pipeline itself.

### Complementary Hardware Paper: HardwareX
For maximum reproducibility impact, a companion HardwareX paper providing the full open-hardware documentation (CAD files, BOM, assembly instructions, validation data) would complement a more methodologically focused flagship paper in one of the venues above (pqac-00000015).

### Conference Option: Proceedings of the Design Society (ICED/DESIGN Conference)
For rapid dissemination, the Design Society conferences (ICED, DESIGN) accept applied LLM-CAD pipeline papers with engineering case studies, as demonstrated by Schüpbach et al. (2025) (pqac-00000016). This is lower-impact but highly amenable to the described contribution.

## 5. Venues to Avoid for This Paper

ACM Transactions on Graphics, CVPR, NeurIPS, ICML, ICLR, and ICCV/ECCV should be avoided unless the manuscript can be reframed around a fundamentally new generative-CAD method (e.g., a novel agentic architecture with broadly applicable improvements over existing text-to-CAD systems, validated on multiple design domains beyond the doser). As currently described—an applied hardware system using generative-CAD as a tool—these venues would reject the paper on scope grounds (pqac-00000003, pqac-00000005, pqac-00000006, pqac-00000007).

contexts.context: This excerpt (Text-to-CadQuery, arXiv) proposes generating executable CadQuery Python code directly from natural language rather than intermediate command sequences. The authors justify choosing CadQuery over alternatives (FreeCAD, OpenSCAD, PythonOCC) because CadQuery is a high-level, pure-Python package that runs without external dependencies and maps well to LLMs' Python-generation abilities. They extend prior datasets (Text2CAD which augments DeepCAD) to produce ~170,000 text–CadQuery pairs and fine-tune six pretrained models (124M–7B parameters), observing scaling gains and improved CAD generation. The paper also notes related industry tools (Zoo) and cites several prior datasets and command-sequence approaches. The excerpt does not list specific publication venues or journals where LLM-based text-to-CAD or code-based generative CAD (using CadQuery, OpenSCAD, or build123d) have appeared, nor does it enumerate which major ML/graphics conferences (NeurIPS, ICML, ICLR, CVPR, SIGGRAPH) have accepted parametric CAD generation papers, so it only partly addresses the user's question.

snippets: Text-to-CadQuery: A New Paradigm for CAD Generation with Scalable Large Model Capabilities
we propose generating CadQuery code directly from text, leveraging the strengths of pretrained LLMs to produce 3D models
we choose CadQuery [44] as the target representation.

Unlike FreeCAD [35] or OpenSCAD [29], which require a full software environment to execute, or PythonOCC, which is too low-level for practical generation, CadQuery is a pure Python package that can be executed directly without any external software dependencies.
industry systems such as Zoo [1] have introduced early Text-to-CAD tools
we build on the Text2CAD [23] dataset, which augments Deep- CAD [45] with natural language descriptions of 3D models.
This results in a new dataset of approximately 170,000 text–CadQuery pairs, enabling direct training for Text-to-CadQuery generation.
We fine-tune six models with parameter sizes ranging from 124 million to 7 billion.

contexts.context: This excerpt is from a submission titled OBJ2CAD that is "Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2026." It situates OBJ2CAD within recent LLM-driven text-to-CAD work (citing Zhang et al., 2024; Xie et al., 2025; Khan et al., 2024) and notes prior datasets used by procedural/code-based approaches, specifically DeepCAD (Wu et al., 2021) and Fusion 360 Gallery (Willis et al., 2021). The excerpt highlights that many prior systems reconstruct geometry via ordered CAD operations — "sketch, extrude, and fillet" — and characterizes this as a brittle "procedural modeling paradigm" that encodes many low-level constraints. OBJ2CAD proposes an object-oriented representation and an "LLM-driven framework that combines top-down planning with bottom-up generation," plus "geometric assembly reasoning" and an interactive refinement loop. The excerpt therefore confirms ICLR as a venue for this kind of work (at least as a submission) and documents the existence of prior code/procedural datasets and methods, but it does not list specific publication venues or journals for CadQuery/OpenSCAD/build123d-based generative CAD work nor enumerate which of NeurIPS, ICML, CVPR, or SIGGRAPH have accepted parametric CAD generation papers. Additional bibliographic search is needed to answer the venues/conference acceptance question fully.

snippets: Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2026
Text-to-CAD has emerged as a promising direction to generate computer-aided design (CAD) programs directly from natural language (Zhang et al., 2024; Xie et al., 2025; Khan et al., 2024).
Most existing work relies on datasets such as DeepCAD (Wu et  al., 2021) and Fusion 360 Gallery (Willis et al., 2021),
operations like sketch, extrude, and fillet.
procedural modeling paradigm
OBJ2CAD represents CAD models as collections of objects, assembled into complex structures, while de-emphasizing redundant low-level constraints and highlighting logical relations between objects.
an LLM-driven framework that combines top-down planning with bottom-up generation
Geometric assembly reasoning that formulates assembly as explicit geometric-mathematical constraints, improving spatial reliability.

contexts.context: This excerpt is an open-access article published in HardwareX describing the Laborem Box, an open-source, scalable platform for remote electronics laboratories. The article includes a specifications table and explicit metadata fields typical for hardware publications: hardware name, subject area, hardware type, an open source license (GNU General Public License v3.0), cost of hardware (429.03 €), and a source file repository DOI (Zenodo). The authors state the platform migrated to open source software and an open source interface box to enable interchangeable PCB "plugs," and emphasize the goal of making a low-cost, simple-to-deploy, open source, and scalable solution for undergraduate remote labs. The paper is intended both to describe hardware and software design and to serve as a guide for duplication and deployment. The excerpt also highlights a broader context: a lack of previous remote labs offering open source hardware and a trend toward using SBCs (Raspberry Pi), Arduinos, and custom PCBs in remote lab implementations.

To date, there has been no remote laboratory offering open source hardware like electronic boards developed to perform practical work.

The Laborem project developed at the technological university institute (IUT) in Bayonne, France, aims to offer remote electronics practical work via a software and hardware platform that is inexpensive, simple to deploy, open source, and scalable.

This paper is intended to describe the hardware and software design of Laborem platform, and to serve as a guide to explain how to duplicate and deploy this system, primarily dedicated to undergraduate students for learning basic electronics.

contexts.context: The excerpt is from an article published in Digital Discovery (Royal Society of Chemistry, 2025) describing advances in low-cost 3D printing and open-source approaches for laboratory automation and self-driving labs. It emphasizes democratizing chemical synthesis and analysis by enabling researchers to download, customize, and fabricate reactionware and instruments using affordable 3D printers and open-source software. Examples cited include modular 3D-printed reactionware for continuous-flow reactions, integrated in-line monitoring (ATR-IR), unified synthesis robots, and low-cost robotically controlled syringe-based reactionware. The text highlights digitizing automated chemical synthesis by defining unit operations (heating, stirring, transfers) in modular, machine-readable formats and building repositories for synthesis designs and data (e.g., ChemSCAD references). Overall, the excerpt indicates Digital Discovery publishes work on digitalization of chemistry, laboratory automation, open hardware/software tools, and methods that enable accessible, reproducible automated chemical synthesis workflows.
contexts.question: What is the scope and aims of Digital Discovery (RSC) journal? Does it accept open hardware contributions for self-driving laboratories? What types of papers does HardwareX accept?

contexts.context: This HardwareX article describes Laborem Box, an open-source, 3D-printable hardware platform for remote electronics labs. The paper is published in HardwareX (HardwareX 11 (2022) e00301) and is released under open-access CC BY with hardware files under GNU GPL v3.0 and a Zenodo source repository (https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.5564369). The authors position Laborem as an inexpensive, scalable, open-source platform intended to let students remotely perform practical electronics work; they note many remote labs use single board computers (SBCs) such as the Raspberry Pi to control equipment. The article emphasizes providing hardware and software design details to enable duplication and deployment, and it references OSHWA-style open-source hardware principles. It also states that, until recently, few remote laboratories offered open-source electronic boards for hands-on practical work. Overall, the excerpt shows HardwareX as a venue that publishes open-source scientific hardware descriptions and illustrates typical elements provided (license, repository, SBC usage, deployment guidance).

To date, there has been no remote laboratory offering open source hardware like electronic boards developed to perform practical work.

The Laborem project developed at the technological university institute (IUT) in Bayonne, France, aims to offer remote electronics practical work via a software and hardware platform that is inexpensive, simple to deploy, open source, and scalable.

This paper is intended to describe the hardware and software design of Laborem platform, and to serve as a guide to explain how to duplicate and deploy this system, primarily dedicated to undergraduate students for learning basic electronics.

contexts.context: The provided excerpt is an accepted manuscript published in the Royal Society of Chemistry journal Digital Discovery (DOI: 10.1039/D4DD00411F). The paper, titled "Democratizing Self-Driving Labs: Advances in Low-Cost 3D Printing for Laboratory Automation," presents an applied hardware contribution focused on converting low-cost Fused Deposition Modelling (FDM) 3D printers into laboratory automation platforms. It frames laboratory automation as a transformative approach to accelerating discovery and highlights 3D printing as a "democratizing force," emphasizing open-source designs that lower cost barriers and enable sharing, modification, and community-driven improvement. The manuscript explicitly discusses the "use of FDM 3D-printed components" to build liquid handlers, robotic arms, automated sample prep and detection systems, and integration with machine learning and AI for experiment planning. The document is peer-reviewed and cited, indicating that Digital Discovery accepts applied, open-hardware descriptions that combine low-cost fabrication, open-source design, and AI/ML-driven experimentation as a methodological component rather than novel AI-method research.

The paper positions this approach as lowering the barrier for non-professionals and easing design-to-manufacturing workflows. The excerpt lists keywords tying the work to Computer-aided design, Design automation, AI, and LLMs, and shows standard journal metadata (DOI, received/accepted dates), indicating IJAMT is a venue that publishes applied research at the intersection of AI, CAD, and manufacturing. snippets: Generative AI meets CAD: enhancing engineering design to manufacturing processes with large language models

This study presents a framework for LLM-assisted CAD that employs advanced artiﬁcial intelligence systems, speciﬁcally GPT-4o model, to provide users with the ability to generate and manipulate 3D models through text commands, visual inputs such as blueprints, images, voice, and any other type of general or professional information about the desired model.

By integrating LLMs with CAD software and incorporating image recognition and voice detection capabilities, we aim to lower the barrier to entry for 3D modeling, making it more user-friendly and accessible to non-professionals.
The proposed framework employs LLM to process user inputs (text, images, and/or voice) and generate the code corresponding the CAD model.
A closed feedback loop ensures that the generated models are reﬁned iteratively until they meet the user’s speciﬁcations.
Keywords Computer-aided design · Design automation · Artiﬁcial intelligence · Large language models · Chat-GPT
The International Journal of Advanced Manufacturing Technology
Received: 22 April 2025 / Accepted: 26 May 2025

contexts.context: The Daareyni et al. (2025) paper presents GAD, an LLM-driven generative CAD framework that integrates CAD modeling, slicing, and manufacturing into a single user interface. The system was validated by comparing user-specified and final printed gear parameters, with observed deviations attributed to the manufacturing process, showing the framework follows design criteria. GAD can interpret images and reproduce general shapes, but the paper notes limitations in achieving fine-detail fidelity and precise dimensional accuracy, especially with limited input or complex geometries. The authors evaluated alternative mid-sized LLMs (Google Gemini 2.5 Pro and OlympicCoder) specifically "to assess their performance in generating SCAD code." Both showed high success rates but long execution times, highlighting trade-offs between model performance and processing efficiency. Future work suggested includes better dimensional extrapolation, handling complex geometries, advanced error-detection/self-correction, and exploring cost- or speed-efficient LLM alternatives or locally trained models. The article references OpenSCAD and several practical tools (Streamlit, Slic3r, Printrun), is published in The International Journal of Advanced Manufacturing Technology, is open-access under a CC BY 4.0 license, and the record notes "This article has 26 citations."

Eng. paper that are directly relevant to generative or deep-learning-driven CAD workflows, but they are from 2019–2020 rather than the 2022–2025 window requested. Notable items listed include: (1) Cunningham et al. (JMD, 2019) on surrogate models for point-cloud decoding; (2) Oh et al. (JMD, 2019) “Deep Generative Design: Integration of Topology Optimization and Generative Models”; (3) Williams et al. (JMD, 2019) on 3D CNNs and additive manufacturing design repositories; and (4) Nie et al. (J. Comput. Inf. Sci. Eng., 2020) on stress-field prediction with CNNs. These are representative earlier papers that bridge generative models/deep learning and CAD/CAE and can serve as foundational references when searching for more recent (2022–2025) work. (same years) for keywords like “generative design,” “deep generative,” “deep learning,” “topology optimization,” and “CAD/CAE integration” will be needed to compile the requested recent papers.

answer.formatted_answer:
Question: Identify and rank the highest-impact peer-reviewed venues (journals and archival conference proceedings) to publish work that leans into the GENERATIVE-CAD aspect of an open-hardware self-driving-laboratory project, in which the mechanical hardware (a multi-powder doser for autonomous AM alloy discovery) is designed via an LLM/AI-driven, code-based generative-CAD pipeline (e.g., CadQuery / build123d / OpenSCAD text-to-CAD, agentic design loops, geometry-kernel verifiers, design-for-additive-manufacturing). Prior literature reviews for this project (the project's own generative-CAD background notes) found that generative-CAD work is published across: ACM Transactions on Graphics (SIGGRAPH / SIGGRAPH Asia), CVPR / ICCV / ECCV and other IEEE/CVF venues, NeurIPS / ICML / ICLR, Computer-Aided Design (Elsevier), Computer-Aided Design and Applications, The International Journal of Advanced Manufacturing Technology, ASME Journal of Mechanical Design, Journal of Computing and Information Science in Engineering (JCISE), Additive Manufacturing, Applied Sciences, Computers in Industry, Advanced Engineering Informatics, and Proceedings of the Design Society. For each candidate venue give: full name, publisher, scope, approximate impact factor / CiteScore / h5-index (for conferences), whether it accepts an applied systems/hardware contribution that uses (rather than purely advances) generative-CAD methods, typical acceptance bar, and a 1-3 sentence justification of fit. Specifically weigh the trade-off that this is an APPLIED hardware/SDL contribution using generative CAD as a design methodology, not a pure ML/graphics methods paper, and flag which high-impact venues would or would not accept it on that basis. Recommend the best high-impact venue(s) that remain reasonably amenable to a hardware-focused, self-driving-lab contribution. Cite venue scope pages and representative recent (2022-2025) generative-CAD papers in each.

# Venue Analysis for a Generative-CAD / Open-Hardware Self-Driving Laboratory Contribution

## 1. The Publication Landscape for Generative-CAD Work

Generative-CAD research—including parametric sequence generation, B-rep synthesis, and LLM-driven text-to-CAD—is published across a broad spectrum of venues. Evidence from recent literature shows that the highest-profile generative-CAD *methods* papers appear in top ML and computer vision venues: CVPR hosted CAD-Llama (Li et al., 2025) for LLM-based parametric CAD generation (li2025cadllamaleveraginglarge pages 8-9), NeurIPS published Text2CAD (Khan et al., 2024) (dong2025histcadgeometricallyconstrained pages 12-12, le2025cadknittercompositionalcad pages 16-17), ICML published SkexGen (Xu et al., 2022) (le2025cadknittercompositionalcad pages 16-17, xu2024brepgenabrep pages 11-12), ICLR has OBJ2CAD-type work under review (houUnknownyearobj2cadobjectorientedtexttocad pages 1-2), and ACM Transactions on Graphics (SIGGRAPH) published BrepGen (Xu et al., 2024) (xu2024brepgenabrep pages 11-12). Applied code-based generative-CAD work using frameworks like CadQuery and OpenSCAD has appeared in engineering journals: Daareyni et al. (2025) published an LLM-to-OpenSCAD pipeline in The International Journal of Advanced Manufacturing Technology (daareyni2025generativeaimeets pages 9-10, daareyni2025generativeaimeets pages 1-2), Schüpbach et al. (2025) published an agentic LLM-CAD framework in Proceedings of the Design Society (gomez2025designautomationtechniques pages 86-89), and the Text-to-CadQuery system (Xie & Ju, 2025) demonstrates CadQuery-specific LLM fine-tuning (xie2505texttocadqueryanew pages 12-15, xie2505texttocadqueryanew pages 10-12).

Separately, self-driving laboratory (SDL) hardware contributions—particularly open-source, 3D-printed, and modular instruments—are concentrated in Digital Discovery (RSC), which has published open-hardware SDL platforms including the digital pipette (Yoshikawa et al., 2023) and democratized 3D-printed lab automation (Doloi et al., 2025) (doloi2025democratizingselfdrivinglabs pages 29-30, doloi2025democratizingselfdrivinglabs pages 1-2), as well as in HardwareX, which accepts detailed open-source hardware descriptions (lavayssiere2022laboremboxa pages 1-2). Other outlets for SDL hardware include Nature Communications, Chemical Reviews, and domain-specific chemistry/materials journals (pelkie2025democratizingselfdrivinglabs pages 16-17, silva2024shapingthefuture pages 12-14, wenzel2023openhardwarefrom pages 25-28).

## 2. The Core Trade-Off: Applied Hardware/SDL vs. Pure Methods

The critical trade-off for this project is that the contribution is primarily an *applied* hardware/SDL system paper—a multi-powder doser for autonomous AM alloy discovery—that *uses* generative-CAD (LLM/AI-driven code-based CAD via CadQuery/build123d/OpenSCAD, with agentic design loops and geometry-kernel verifiers) as a design methodology, rather than advancing generative-CAD methods per se. This distinction fundamentally shapes venue selection:

**Venues that would NOT accept the paper** (high-impact but methods-focused): ACM TOG/SIGGRAPH, CVPR, ICCV/ECCV, NeurIPS, ICML, and ICLR all publish generative-CAD work, but they require the primary contribution to be a novel ML, vision, or graphics *method*. An applied paper that uses LLM-CAD to design hardware but does not advance the underlying model architecture, training procedure, or representation would almost certainly be desk-rejected at these venues (li2025cadllamaleveraginglarge pages 8-9, xu2024brepgenabrep pages 11-12).

**Venues that WOULD accept the paper** (applied-systems-friendly): Digital Discovery, IJAMT, ASME JMD/JCISE, Additive Manufacturing, Advanced Engineering Informatics, Computers in Industry, and Proceedings of the Design Society all have track records of publishing applied AI-for-design or hardware/SDL contributions, provided there is sufficient methodological or engineering novelty (doloi2025democratizingselfdrivinglabs pages 1-2, daareyni2025generativeaimeets pages 9-10, gomez2025designautomationtechniques pages 86-89, man2025fromsketchto pages 84-84).

## 3. Ranked Venue Table

The following table ranks all candidate venues by overall fit for the described contribution, with impact as a secondary factor.

| Rank | Venue full name | Publisher | Approximate Impact Factor / CiteScore / h5-index | Scope summary | Accepts applied hardware/SDL contribution? | Acceptance bar | Representative recent generative-CAD paper (2022-2025) | Fit justification |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Digital Discovery | Royal Society of Chemistry (RSC) | IF ~6.2; CiteScore ~10 | Publishes digital, automated, AI-enabled chemistry/materials workflows, including self-driving labs, open hardware, and low-cost automation. | Yes | Medium | Yoshikawa et al., “Digital pipette: open hardware for liquid transfer in self-driving laboratories” (2023); Doloi et al., “Democratizing Self-Driving Labs: Advances in Low-Cost 3D Printing for Laboratory Automation” (2025) (doloi2025democratizingselfdrivinglabs pages 1-2, doloi2025democratizingselfdrivinglabs pages 29-30) | Best overall fit if the paper foregrounds the open-hardware SDL contribution and treats generative-CAD as the enabling design methodology. It already publishes SDL hardware, 3D-printed lab automation, and community-facing open systems papers, so a multi-powder doser for autonomous AM alloy discovery is squarely in-scope. |
| 2 | Additive Manufacturing | Elsevier | IF ~11; CiteScore ~19 | Top AM journal covering materials, processes, design-for-AM, AM systems, and integrated design/manufacturing workflows. | Partial | High | AM-driven generative design / DfAM papers are common; e.g., ML-assisted DfAM and topology/generative-design studies cited in the AM literature review stream (Watson 2023 contextual fit via AM-focused generative design) | Highest-impact manufacturing venue still plausibly amenable if the paper emphasizes the doser as novel AM-enabling hardware for alloy discovery and shows that the generative-CAD pipeline materially improved printability, modularity, or DfAM performance. Less ideal if the manuscript is mostly about SDL architecture rather than AM outcomes. |
| 3 | The International Journal of Advanced Manufacturing Technology | Springer Nature | IF ~3.4; CiteScore ~8 | Broad manufacturing journal spanning design, process planning, automation, robotics, CAD/CAM, and applied AI in manufacturing. | Yes | Medium-High | Daareyni et al., “Generative AI meets CAD: enhancing engineering design to manufacturing processes with large language models” (2025); Watson et al., “Generative design of space frames for additive manufacturing technology” (2023) (daareyni2025generativeaimeets pages 9-10, daareyni2025generativeaimeets pages 1-2) | Very strong fit for a paper that integrates LLM-driven code-based CAD with actual fabrication hardware and manufacturing validation. It has direct precedent for OpenSCAD/LLM CAD pipelines and for generative-design-for-AM papers, making it one of the safest journal targets. |
| 4 | Journal of Mechanical Design | ASME | IF ~3.3; CiteScore ~6 | Flagship engineering-design journal focused on methods, theories, and systems for design synthesis, automation, and computational design. | Partial | High | Regenwetter et al., “Deep Generative Models in Engineering Design: A Review” (2022); GenCAD=3D (2025) noted in recent JMD-linked literature (man2025fromsketchto pages 84-84) | Excellent fit if the paper makes a genuine engineering-design-method contribution: e.g., an agentic generative-CAD workflow with verification, parameterized modularity, and DfAM-aware design rules validated on the doser. It is less suitable if the main novelty is the hardware build itself rather than the design methodology. |
| 5 | Journal of Computing and Information Science in Engineering (JCISE) | ASME | IF ~3.1; CiteScore ~6 | Covers computational methods, CAD/CAE/CAM, design automation, geometric computing, and AI/ML for engineering information and systems. | Partial | Medium-High | Recent JCISE/JMD-linked LLM4CAD and CAD-automation literature cluster; code-generation/engineering informatics framing fits the venue’s computational-design remit (man2025fromsketchto pages 84-84) | Strong choice when the core claim is a reusable computational pipeline for text/code-based CAD generation, verification, and design-space exploration, with the hardware as the case study. More amenable than pure ML venues, but still expects a clear computational contribution. |
| 6 | Computer-Aided Design | Elsevier | IF ~3.0; CiteScore ~7 | Core CAD journal for geometric modeling, design automation, computational representations, and CAD-related AI methods. | Partial | High | Simplification of 3D CAD models using GANs (2023, cited as unobtainable in search); broader generative-CAD lineage including B-rep/sequence modeling is central to the venue ecosystem (xie2505texttocadqueryanew pages 12-15, xu2024brepgenabrep pages 11-12) | Strong venue if the manuscript leans into the generative-CAD pipeline itself: code representations, geometry-kernel verification, parametric editability, and CAD automation. Weaker if the paper is mainly an SDL/open-hardware system paper with minimal CAD-method novelty. |
| 7 | Advanced Engineering Informatics | Elsevier | IF ~8.0; CiteScore ~15 | Publishes AI, knowledge-based systems, digital engineering, and informatics for design, construction, and manufacturing. | Yes | High | Generative/AI-for-design and engineering-informatics papers are common; recent agentic/LLM engineering design pipelines are highly consonant with venue norms | Very attractive if the paper stresses the end-to-end digital thread: LLM-driven CAD, validation, knowledge capture, hardware documentation, and closed-loop laboratory deployment. It is more systems-friendly than pure CAD journals, while still rewarding methodological novelty. |
| 8 | Computers in Industry | Elsevier | IF ~10.0; CiteScore ~17 | Focuses on industrially relevant computer-based methods, digitalization, automation, and deployable systems in engineering/manufacturing. | Yes | High | Applied AI/CAD/manufacturing systems papers are typical; recent industrial CAD-generation and automation work fits its translational orientation | Good high-impact option if the doser is presented as a deployable industrial-style digital engineering system rather than a purely academic design-method paper. Better for demonstrable workflow integration and usability than for algorithmic CAD novelty per se. |
| 9 | Proceedings of the Design Society | Cambridge University Press / Design Society | archival proceedings; no standard JIF; CiteScore modest | Archival design-research proceedings covering engineering design methods, tools, and emerging AI-enabled design workflows. | Yes | Medium | Schüpbach et al., “From text to design: a framework to leverage LLM agents for automated CAD generation” (2025) (gomez2025designautomationtechniques pages 86-89) | One of the clearest venues for agentic generative-CAD workflows with a real engineering case study. Lower impact than the best journals, but notably more amenable to an applied paper where the methodological contribution is modest yet meaningful. |

*Table: This table ranks peer-reviewed venues for a generative-CAD plus open-hardware self-driving-lab paper, prioritizing fit for an applied hardware/SDL contribution over raw venue prestige. It distinguishes journals and conferences that are realistically receptive from high-impact ML/graphics venues that publish generative CAD but would likely reject an applied systems paper.*

## 4. Top Recommendations

Based on the analysis, the recommended publication strategy depends on how the manuscript is framed:

### Primary Recommendation: Digital Discovery (RSC)
Digital Discovery is the single venue that sits at the intersection of SDL open hardware, AI-driven automation, and accessible 3D-printed instruments for materials discovery. It has published open-hardware SDL platforms (doloi2025democratizingselfdrivinglabs pages 29-30, doloi2025democratizingselfdrivinglabs pages 1-2, maffettoneUnknownyearwhatismissing pages 1-3), has a growing IF (~6.2), and would welcome a paper that foregrounds the multi-powder doser as an AI-designed, open-source SDL module for autonomous AM alloy discovery. The generative-CAD pipeline would serve as the enabling methodology rather than needing to be a standalone ML contribution.

### High-Impact Alternative: The International Journal of Advanced Manufacturing Technology (Springer)
IJAMT has direct precedent for LLM-driven code-based CAD work applied to manufacturing: Daareyni et al. (2025) published an LLM-to-OpenSCAD pipeline with manufacturing validation in this exact journal (daareyni2025generativeaimeets pages 9-10, daareyni2025generativeaimeets pages 1-2). A paper framing the doser as a case study in LLM-driven design-for-additive-manufacturing with fabrication and functional validation would be highly competitive here.

### If Emphasizing Design Methodology: ASME Journal of Mechanical Design or JCISE
If the manuscript can make a genuine methodological contribution regarding the agentic design loop, geometry-kernel verification, or DfAM-aware generative-CAD workflow, JMD or JCISE would provide higher prestige within the engineering design community. JMD has published the deep generative models review by Regenwetter et al. (2022) and GenCAD=3D (2025) (man2025fromsketchto pages 84-84), establishing precedent for AI-enabled design synthesis work.

### If Emphasizing Manufacturing/AM Outcomes: Additive Manufacturing (Elsevier)
With an IF of ~11, Additive Manufacturing is the highest-impact manufacturing-specific venue. However, the paper would need to center on AM-relevant contributions (novel powder dosing mechanisms, multi-material process capabilities, alloy discovery results) rather than the CAD pipeline itself.

### Complementary Hardware Paper: HardwareX
For maximum reproducibility impact, a companion HardwareX paper providing the full open-hardware documentation (CAD files, BOM, assembly instructions, validation data) would complement a more methodologically focused flagship paper in one of the venues above (lavayssiere2022laboremboxa pages 1-2).

### Conference Option: Proceedings of the Design Society (ICED/DESIGN Conference)
For rapid dissemination, the Design Society conferences (ICED, DESIGN) accept applied LLM-CAD pipeline papers with engineering case studies, as demonstrated by Schüpbach et al. (2025) (gomez2025designautomationtechniques pages 86-89). This is lower-impact but highly amenable to the described contribution.

## 5. Venues to Avoid for This Paper

ACM Transactions on Graphics, CVPR, NeurIPS, ICML, ICLR, and ICCV/ECCV should be avoided unless the manuscript can be reframed around a fundamentally new generative-CAD method (e.g., a novel agentic architecture with broadly applicable improvements over existing text-to-CAD systems, validated on multiple design domains beyond the doser). As currently described—an applied hardware system using generative-CAD as a tool—these venues would reject the paper on scope grounds (li2025cadllamaleveraginglarge pages 8-9, le2025cadknittercompositionalcad pages 16-17, xu2024brepgenabrep pages 11-12, houUnknownyearobj2cadobjectorientedtexttocad pages 1-2).

info.query: Identify and rank the highest-impact peer-reviewed venues (journals and archival conference proceedings) to publish work that leans into the GENERATIVE-CAD aspect of an open-hardware self-driving-laboratory project, in which the mechanical hardware (a multi-powder doser for autonomous AM alloy discovery) is designed via an LLM/AI-driven, code-based generative-CAD pipeline (e.g., CadQuery / build123d / OpenSCAD text-to-CAD, agentic design loops, geometry-kernel verifiers, design-for-additive-manufacturing). Prior literature reviews for this project (the project's own generative-CAD background notes) found that generative-CAD work is published across: ACM Transactions on Graphics (SIGGRAPH / SIGGRAPH Asia), CVPR / ICCV / ECCV and other IEEE/CVF venues, NeurIPS / ICML / ICLR, Computer-Aided Design (Elsevier), Computer-Aided Design and Applications, The International Journal of Advanced Manufacturing Technology, ASME Journal of Mechanical Design, Journal of Computing and Information Science in Engineering (JCISE), Additive Manufacturing, Applied Sciences, Computers in Industry, Advanced Engineering Informatics, and Proceedings of the Design Society. For each candidate venue give: full name, publisher, scope, approximate impact factor / CiteScore / h5-index (for conferences), whether it accepts an applied systems/hardware contribution that uses (rather than purely advances) generative-CAD methods, typical acceptance bar, and a 1-3 sentence justification of fit. Specifically weigh the trade-off that this is an APPLIED hardware/SDL contribution using generative CAD as a design methodology, not a pure ML/graphics methods paper, and flag which high-impact venues would or would not accept it on that basis. Recommend the best high-impact venue(s) that remain reasonably amenable to a hardware-focused, self-driving-lab contribution. Cite venue scope pages and representative recent (2022-2025) generative-CAD papers in each.
